Minimum Slack Priority Rule (MSP):
Step 1: Define the problem inputs: a set of activities with durations, precedence constraints, and renewable resource requirements, along with resource capacities.
Step 2: Calculate the earliest start time (EST) and latest finish time (LFT) for each activity using forward and backward passes, ignoring resource constraints.
Step 3: Compute the total slack (or total float) for each activity as LFT - EST - duration.
Step 4: Initialize the project schedule: set the current time t = 0, and create sets for scheduled activities, in-progress activities, and available activities (those with all predecessors scheduled).
Step 5: At each scheduling time t, identify all available activities that are not yet scheduled and whose resource demands do not exceed available capacities when considered with currently in-progress activities.
Step 6: From the set of available and resource-feasible activities, select the activity with the minimum total slack (MSP rule). Break ties arbitrarily or using a secondary rule (e.g., shortest duration).
Step 7: Schedule the selected activity to start at time t, update resource usage, and move it from available to in-progress.
Step 8: Advance time t to the next completion event (when any in-progress activity finishes). Update resource availability and move completed activities to the scheduled set.
Step 9: Update the set of available activities by adding any new activities whose all predecessors are now scheduled.
Step 10: Repeat steps 5-9 until all activities are scheduled. The project makespan is the maximum finish time among all activities.
Greatest Resource Demand Priority Rule (:
Step 1: Define the problem inputs: a set of activities with durations, precedence constraints, and renewable resource requirements, along with resource capacities.
Step 2: Initialize the project schedule: set the current time t = 0, and create sets for scheduled activities, in-progress activities, and available activities (those with all predecessors scheduled).
Step 3: At each scheduling time t, identify all available activities that are not yet scheduled and whose resource demands do not exceed available capacities when considered with currently in-progress activities.
Step 4: For each available and resource-feasible activity, compute its total resource demand. This is typically the sum of its resource requirements across all resource types, optionally weighted by resource criticality or capacity.
Step 5: From the set of available and resource-feasible activities, select the activity with the greatest total resource demand (GRD rule). Break ties arbitrarily or using a secondary rule (e.g., longest duration).
Step 6: Schedule the selected activity to start at time t, update resource usage, and move it from available to in-progress.
Step 7: Advance time t to the next completion event (when any in-progress activity finishes). Update resource availability and move completed activities to the scheduled set.
Step 8: Update the set of available activities by adding any new activities whose all predecessors are now scheduled.
Step 9: Repeat steps 3-8 until all activities are scheduled. The project makespan is the maximum finish time among all activities.
Most Successors Priority Rule (MSP):
Step 1: Define the problem inputs: a set of activities with durations, precedence constraints, and renewable resource requirements, along with resource capacities.
Step 2: For each activity, compute the number of its total successors in the project network (including direct and indirect successors).
Step 3: Initialize the project schedule: set the current time t = 0, and create sets for scheduled activities, in-progress activities, and available activities (those with all predecessors scheduled).
Step 4: At each scheduling time t, identify all available activities that are not yet scheduled and whose resource demands do not exceed available capacities when considered with currently in-progress activities.
Step 5: From the set of available and resource-feasible activities, select the activity with the greatest number of total successors (MSP rule). Break ties arbitrarily or using a secondary rule (e.g., greatest resource demand).
Step 6: Schedule the selected activity to start at time t, update resource usage, and move it from available to in-progress.
Step 7: Advance time t to the next completion event (when any in-progress activity finishes). Update resource availability and move completed activities to the scheduled set.
Step 8: Update the set of available activities by adding any new activities whose all predecessors are now scheduled.
Step 9: Repeat steps 4-8 until all activities are scheduled. The project makespan is the maximum finish time among all activities.
Earliest Start Time Priority Rule (EST):
Step 1: Define the problem inputs: a set of activities with durations, precedence constraints, and renewable resource requirements, along with resource capacities.
Step 2: Calculate the earliest start time (EST) for each activity using a forward pass, ignoring resource constraints.
Step 3: Initialize the project schedule: set the current time t = 0, and create sets for scheduled activities, in-progress activities, and available activities (those with all predecessors scheduled).
Step 4: At each scheduling time t, identify all available activities that are not yet scheduled and whose resource demands do not exceed available capacities when considered with currently in-progress activities.
Step 5: From the set of available and resource-feasible activities, select the activity with the smallest earliest start time (EST rule). Break ties arbitrarily or using a secondary rule (e.g., shortest duration).
Step 6: Schedule the selected activity to start at time t, update resource usage, and move it from available to in-progress.
Step 7: Advance time t to the next completion event (when any in-progress activity finishes). Update resource availability and move completed activities to the scheduled set.
Step 8: Update the set of available activities by adding any new activities whose all predecessors are now scheduled.
Step 9: Repeat steps 4-8 until all activities are scheduled. The project makespan is the maximum finish time among all activities.
Earliest Finish Time Priority Rule (EFT):
Step 1: Define the problem inputs: a set of activities with durations, precedence constraints, and renewable resource requirements, along with resource capacities.
Step 2: Calculate the earliest start time (EST) for each activity using a forward pass, ignoring resource constraints, then compute earliest finish time (EFT) as EST + duration.
Step 3: Initialize the project schedule: set the current time t = 0, and create sets for scheduled activities, in-progress activities, and available activities (those with all predecessors scheduled).
Step 4: At each scheduling time t, identify all available activities that are not yet scheduled and whose resource demands do not exceed available capacities when considered with currently in-progress activities.
Step 5: From the set of available and resource-feasible activities, select the activity with the smallest earliest finish time (EFT rule). Break ties arbitrarily or using a secondary rule (e.g., smallest activity index).
Step 6: Schedule the selected activity to start at time t, update resource usage, and move it from available to in-progress.
Step 7: Advance time t to the next completion event (when any in-progress activity finishes). Update resource availability and move completed activities to the scheduled set.
Step 8: Update the set of available activities by adding any new activities whose all predecessors are now scheduled.
Step 9: Repeat steps 4-8 until all activities are scheduled. The project makespan is the maximum finish time among all activities.
Latest Finish Time Priority Rule (LFT):
Step 1: Define the problem inputs: a set of activities with durations, precedence constraints, and renewable resource requirements, along with resource capacities.
Step 2: Calculate the latest finish time (LFT) for each activity using a backward pass from a project deadline (or critical path length), ignoring resource constraints.
Step 3: Initialize the project schedule: set the current time t = 0, and create sets for scheduled activities, in-progress activities, and available activities (those with all predecessors scheduled).
Step 4: At each scheduling time t, identify all available activities that are not yet scheduled and whose resource demands do not exceed available capacities when considered with currently in-progress activities.
Step 5: From the set of available and resource-feasible activities, select the activity with the smallest latest finish time (LFT rule). Break ties arbitrarily or using a secondary rule (e.g., shortest duration).
Step 6: Schedule the selected activity to start at time t, update resource usage, and move it from available to in-progress.
Step 7: Advance time t to the next completion event (when any in-progress activity finishes). Update resource availability and move completed activities to the scheduled set.
Step 8: Update the set of available activities by adding any new activities whose all predecessors are now scheduled.
Step 9: Repeat steps 4-8 until all activities are scheduled. The project makespan is the maximum finish time among all activities.
Shortest Processing Time Priority Rule (:
Step 1: Define the problem inputs: a set of activities with durations, precedence constraints, and renewable resource requirements, along with resource capacities.
Step 2: Initialize the project schedule: set the current time t = 0, and create sets for scheduled activities, in-progress activities, and available activities (those with all predecessors scheduled).
Step 3: At each scheduling time t, identify all available activities that are not yet scheduled and whose resource demands do not exceed available capacities when considered with currently in-progress activities.
Step 4: From the set of available and resource-feasible activities, select the activity with the shortest processing time (SPT rule). Break ties arbitrarily or using a secondary rule (e.g., smallest activity index).
Step 5: Schedule the selected activity to start at time t, update resource usage, and move it from available to in-progress.
Step 6: Advance time t to the next completion event (when any in-progress activity finishes). Update resource availability and move completed activities to the scheduled set.
Step 7: Update the set of available activities by adding any new activities whose all predecessors are now scheduled.
Step 8: Repeat steps 3-7 until all activities are scheduled. The project makespan is the maximum finish time among all activities.
Longest Processing Time Priority Rule (L:
Step 1: Define the problem inputs: a set of activities with durations, precedence constraints, and renewable resource requirements, along with resource capacities.
Step 2: Initialize the project schedule: set the current time t = 0, and create sets for scheduled activities, in-progress activities, and available activities (those with all predecessors scheduled).
Step 3: At each scheduling time t, identify all available activities that are not yet scheduled and whose resource demands do not exceed available capacities when considered with currently in-progress activities.
Step 4: From the set of available and resource-feasible activities, select the activity with the longest processing time (LPT rule). Break ties arbitrarily or using a secondary rule (e.g., greatest resource demand).
Step 5: Schedule the selected activity to start at time t, update resource usage, and move it from available to in-progress.
Step 6: Advance time t to the next completion event (when any in-progress activity finishes). Update resource availability and move completed activities to the scheduled set.
Step 7: Update the set of available activities by adding any new activities whose all predecessors are now scheduled.
Step 8: Repeat steps 3-7 until all activities are scheduled. The project makespan is the maximum finish time among all activities.
Resource Scheduling Method (RSM):
Step 1: Define the problem inputs: a set of activities with durations, precedence constraints, and renewable resource requirements, along with resource capacities.
Step 2: Calculate the earliest start time (EST) and latest finish time (LFT) for each activity using forward and backward passes, ignoring resource constraints.
Step 3: Compute the total slack for each activity as LFT - EST - duration.
Step 4: Initialize the project schedule: set the current time t = 0, and create sets for scheduled activities, in-progress activities, and available activities (those with all predecessors scheduled).
Step 5: At each scheduling time t, identify all available activities that are not yet scheduled and whose resource demands do not exceed available capacities when considered with currently in-progress activities.
Step 6: If multiple activities are resource-feasible, compute a resource scheduling metric for each (e.g., resource utilization or conflict score).
Step 7: Select the activity that minimizes future resource conflicts or maximizes resource utilization (RSM rule). This often involves looking ahead at resource profiles.
Step 8: Schedule the selected activity to start at time t, update resource usage, and move it from available to in-progress.
Step 9: Advance time t to the next completion event (when any in-progress activity finishes). Update resource availability and move completed activities to the scheduled set.
Step 10: Update the set of available activities by adding any new activities whose all predecessors are now scheduled.
Step 11: Repeat steps 5-10 until all activities are scheduled. The project makespan is the maximum finish time among all activities.

Step 1: Define the problem inputs: a set of activities with durations, precedence constraints, and renewable resource requirements, along with resource capacities.
Step 2: Calculate the latest finish time (LFT) for each activity using a backward pass from a project deadline (or critical path length), ignoring resource constraints.

Step 4: At each scheduling time t, identify all available activities that are not yet scheduled and whose resource demands do not exceed available capacities when considered with currently in-progress activities.
Step 5: From the set of available and resource-feasible activities, select the activity with the minimum latest finish time (LFT rule). Break ties arbitrarily or using a secondary rule (e.g., shortest duration).
Step 6: Schedule the selected activity to start at time t, update resource usage, and move it from available to in-progress.
Step 7: Advance time t to the next completion event (when any in-progress activity finishes). Update resource availability and move completed activities to the scheduled set.
Step 8: Update the set of available activities by adding any new activities whose all predecessors are now scheduled.
Step 9: Repeat steps 4-8 until all activities are scheduled. The project makespan is the maximum finish time among all activities.
